references.title: Detecting students-at-risk in computer programming classes with learning analytics from students’ digital footprints

extracted.Data sources:
Student Characteristics, 
Prior Academic History, LMS logs, Submission Records
extracted.Features: [Distance, CAO Points, MAth LC, Avg. Grade, Exam Course Year, Results Wx, Cumulative Wx, Lab Attendance Wx, Time Wx, Lab Access Wx, Hour Access Wx, Checking Wx, On/of Campus Wx, Week(end) Wx]
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Demographic variables were not considered as not to perpetuate biases stemming from previous cohorts.

Every week, a new set of behavioral features was extracted. Thus, model at week 5 includes all features for Model in Week 4 and the features that are specific for week 5.
extracted.Models: kNN, Decision Tree, Random Forest, Logistic Regression, Linear SVM, Gaussian SVM
extracted.Comments:
Authors create multiple models, one per course. For this paper the only published results are from a CS2 Course.

A prediction is done every week. Ideal model was found to be kNN between Week 5 and Week 6.

Authors reach out to learners flagged as at-risk to provide some addtional feedback.
extracted.Limitations:
Data is publsiuehd for a single course. 

Does not explore potential features extracted from additional sources such as videos or speech.

Small dataset.

extracted.Initialization Method: Recommendations are constructed based on a knowledge graph based on professor’s notion of important concepts.
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
The recommendations are a part of intervention.

At the end of each week, the system sends an e-mail notification to students with their progress and, if learner failed at submission.
extracted.Recommendation types: Most similar successful submission from top student.
extracted.Evaluation: {% of Corrected Suggestions: 0.1160, Differential improvement for Opting-in: 0.1605}
extracted.Comments:
Participation in the intervention work was considered optional - After taking first exam, learners were offered the chance of receiving interventions vs not receiving them.

Authors measured “improvement” between students who opted in vs students who opted out:  All students performed badly on second exam but the decrease for students who opted-in was, at least, smaller.

Positive feedback for interventions.
extracted.Limitations:
Interventions are aimed for students flagged as likely to fail → Vulnerable to Misclassifications.

Requires more data for better recommendations.

annotations.text: Data about students can be extracted from multiple sources in an HE context. Combining data from multiple sources is essencial to create an accurate picture of students.

Paper presents a system that automatically students at risk and provides personalized adaptive feedback in programming courses. Authors combine data from multiple sources to create a system that predicts whether they are at-risk or not. These predictions are generated weekly.

More specifically, students under the system praised it and heavily recommended its usage by subsequent students. Also, students who accepted the personalized recommendations also performed better than their peers.
